title: Universal thermokinetic decomposition of short-time information fluctuations
abstract: Biological, artificial, and physical systems dissipate energy to accurately transmit information. While tools of information theory have been used to characterize information-processing capabilities, how reliably this information is acquired along individual trajectories, and which aspects require a thermodynamic cost, is an open question. In this work, we focus on the stochastic predictability of an arbitrary Langevin dynamics, defined as the pointwise mutual information between the current and future states of a system. We show that the fluctuations of predictability obey a universal thermokinetic decomposition at short times, which reveals that information fluctuations are suppressed by energy dissipation and become stronger with increased dynamical activity. Remarkably, we find that the average predictability, i.e., the short-time mutual information, does not carry any dependence on the underlying thermodynamic and kinetic features. Thus, the role of dissipation at short times is not to enhance information, but to reduce its fluctuations. Such dissipative control is effective only when instantiated by nonlinear operations. Moreover, energy consumption governs short- and long-time precision in stochastic oscillators through structurally different mechanisms that can be independently tuned. Our decomposition offers a fundamental thermodynamic basis for understanding the reliability of information transmission in nonequilibrium systems, the constraints on precision in biological systems, and the design of energy-limited control strategies.
summary: This work develops a universal decomposition showing that information fluctuations in stochastic systems are governed by a balance between energy dissipation and dynamical activity. While the average predictability is robust, the variance of this predictability is fundamentally controlled by thermodynamic quantities like entropy production. This offers a new thermodynamic lens to analyze information reliability in complex, non-equilibrium physical and biological processes.
main_problem: To understand how energy dissipation and dynamical activity influence the reliability of information acquisition in physical systems by characterizing the fluctuations of short-time information predictability.
main_result: Information fluctuations exhibit a universal thermokinetic decomposition, showing that thermodynamics controls the *fluctuations* of predictability, while the average predictability is independent of underlying thermodynamic details.
method: The analysis focuses on the short-time behavior of the pointwise mutual information (pMI) using the Moment Generating Function (MGF) derived from the Langevin dynamics.
model_or_system: The study applies to arbitrary Langevin dynamics for a system with N degrees of freedom, exemplified by Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes, Brownian motors, and stochastic oscillators near a Hopf bifurcation.
key_observables: Pointwise Mutual Information (pMI), Information Fluctuations ($F_	au$), Average Predictability ($I_	au$), Entropy Production Rate ($\Sigma$), and Stochastic Action ($T$).
important_parameters: Short time ($	au$), dimensionality ($N$), and the strength of dissipation/driving forces.
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the assumption of stationarity for the process, though extensions to non-stationary and multiplicative noise are mentioned.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the relationship between information fluctuations ($F_	au$) and thermodynamic quantities ($\Sigma, T$), showing how dissipation suppresses fluctuations while dynamical activity influences them.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical framework by defining pMI and analyzing its short-time MGF. It then derives the universal decomposition for $F_	au$, contrasting it with the insensitivity of $I_	au$ to thermodynamic details, and applies this to various physical models.
why_interesting: The finding that thermodynamic constraints govern information *fluctuations* but not the average information transfer provides a novel, fundamental thermodynamic basis for understanding precision limits in non-equilibrium systems, relevant to open quantum systems modeling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The state of a stochastic process described by Eq. (1) evolves from x0 to xτ, fluctuating in time and gener- ating a set of stochastic trajectories in a manifold M. These fluctuations shape the information shared between present and future states, which is captured by the distribution of the pointwise mutual information (pMI) between x0 and xτ, iτ. While a dissipative driving f does not impact the aver- age pMI, Iτ, higher dissipation is in general able to reduce the short-time fluctuations of information, Fτ, quantified by the variance of the pMI. (b) For a one-dimensional Ornstein- Uhlenbeck (OU) process with F(x) = −kx, Iτ diverges loga- rithmically as τ →0, while Fτ...

FIG. 2. (a-c) Steady-state distribution pst of a particle in a one-dimensional periodic potential with a nonequilibrium driving f. The entropy production Σ increases with f, while the varentropy FH(−log pst) decreases as the distribution be- comes more uniform. (d) Information fluctuations Fτ are sup- pressed by the nonequilibrium driving, up to the theoretical minimum (gray dashed line). Our short-time theory (solid lines) is well-matched by numerical simulations (circles) even for relatively large values of τ. In these panels, k = 2 and D = 1.
